upgradeInfo.migrationMarkdown:
Introducing GPT-5.4

Codex just got an upgrade with GPT-5.4, our most capable model for professional work. It outperforms prior models while being more token efficient, with notable improvements on long-running tasks, tool calling, computer use, and frontend development.

Learn more: https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-4

You can always keep using GPT-5.3-Codex if you prefer.

content.text: Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is
Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is

msg.message: Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is

content.text:
Canada is a vast North American country known for its immense geography, political stability, cultural diversity, and strong public institutions. It is the second-largest country in the world by total area, stretching from the Atlantic Ocean in the east to the Pacific Ocean in the west and up into the Arctic Ocean in the north. Because of this scale, Canada contains an extraordinary range of landscapes: rugged coastlines, prairie grasslands, dense boreal forests, major mountain ranges such as the Rockies, thousands of lakes, Arctic tundra, and some of the planet’s largest freshwater systems.

Canada is a federal parliamentary democracy and a constitutional monarchy. Its head of state is the monarch of Canada, represented domestically by the governor general, while the head of government is the prime minister. The country is divided into ten provinces and three territories. The provinces have significant constitutional powers, especially in areas like health care, education, and natural resources, while the territories operate with powers delegated by the federal government. This balance between federal and regional authority is a major feature of Canadian political life.

The capital city is Ottawa, located in Ontario, but Canada’s largest cities include Toronto, Montreal, Vancouver, Calgary, Edmonton, and others. These urban centers are economically and culturally important, but much of Canada’s identity is also tied to smaller communities, remote northern settlements, and regional distinctions. Atlantic Canada, Quebec, Ontario, the Prairies, British Columbia, and the North each have distinct historical, linguistic, economic, and cultural characteristics.

One of the defining features of Canada is bilingualism at the federal level. English and French are the country’s official languages, and this reflects a long historical relationship between British and French colonial influence. Quebec is the center of Francophone culture in Canada and has a strong provincial identity, shaped by language, law, religion, and nationalism. French-speaking communities also exist in other provinces, especially New Brunswick, which is officially bilingual, and parts of Ontario and Manitoba.

Canada’s history is inseparable from Indigenous peoples, including First Nations, Inuit, and Métis communities, who lived on these lands for thousands of years before European colonization. Indigenous nations developed complex societies, governance systems, trade networks, and cultural traditions long before the arrival of Europeans. Modern Canada has been increasingly shaped by efforts to address the legacy of colonization, especially the residential school system, land dispossession, treaty violations, and broader systemic injustice. Reconciliation has become a major national issue, though it remains incomplete and often contested in practice.

European colonization began primarily through French and British expansion. France established settlements along the St. Lawrence River and in other parts of what became Canada. Britain later gained control over much of New France after the Seven Years’ War. Over time, the British colonies in North America evolved into a self-governing dominion. Confederation in 1867 united Ontario, Quebec, New Brunswick, and Nova Scotia into the Dominion of Canada, which later expanded westward and northward. Full sovereignty developed gradually over time through constitutional evolution rather than a single revolutionary break.

Canada is often described as multicultural, and that is a central part of its national self-image. Immigration has played a major role in the country’s development, particularly in the 20th and 21st centuries. Large immigrant communities from Asia, Europe, the Caribbean, Africa, the Middle East, and Latin America have shaped Canadian cities and public life. Official multiculturalism became federal policy, encouraging the preservation of cultural identity alongside civic participation. In practice, this has produced a society that is diverse, relatively pluralistic, and generally supportive of immigration, though it also faces tensions around integration, discrimination, and national identity.

The economy of Canada is highly developed and resource-rich. It combines a large service sector with strong industries in finance, technology, manufacturing, mining, forestry, agriculture, fisheries, and energy. Canada is a major producer of oil, natural gas, uranium, potash, timber, and various minerals. It is also a major agricultural exporter, especially of wheat, canola, and other grains. Trade with the United States is central to the Canadian economy, given the long shared border and deep integration of supply chains. At the same time, Canada has increasingly pursued trade relationships with Europe and Asia.

Canada is widely recognized for its public health care system, though the reality is more accurately described as a publicly funded system administered largely by provinces. Access to medically necessary physician and hospital services is generally covered, which has become a core point of national identity. However, the system also faces major pressures, including long wait times, regional disparities, staff shortages, and growing demand from an aging population.

Education levels in Canada are high, and the country has a strong network of public schools, colleges, and universities. Institutions such as the University of Toronto, McGill University, the University of British Columbia, and others are well known internationally. Public education is primarily a provincial responsibility, which means systems vary somewhat by region, but overall literacy, post-secondary participation, and educational attainment are strong by international standards.

Culturally, Canada often balances influences from Britain, France, the United States, and its own regional and Indigenous traditions. Canadian literature, music, film, television, and visual art reflect this layered identity. Writers such as Margaret Atwood, Alice Munro, and Michael Ondaatje have international prominence. Canadian musicians and performers have had outsized global influence across genres. At the same time, there is a persistent concern in Canada about cultural proximity to the United States, especially in media and entertainment, leading to long-standing efforts to support domestic cultural production.

Canada is also strongly associated with nature and outdoor life. National parks such as Banff, Jasper, Gros Morne, and many others are central to its image. Wildlife including moose, bears, beavers, caribou, and whales are commonly associated with the country. Winter is an important part of Canadian identity in many regions, shaping everything from transportation and architecture to sports and recreation. Ice hockey holds a special symbolic place, though Canada is also deeply engaged with sports such as lacrosse, soccer, basketball, curling, and skiing.

Climate varies enormously across the country. Southern regions where most people live experience four distinct seasons, while northern areas can have extremely long, severe winters and short summers. Climate change has become a significant issue in Canada, especially in relation to wildfires, Arctic warming, melting permafrost, flooding, and changing ecosystems. This creates an especially sharp policy tension because Canada is both vulnerable to climate impacts and economically tied to fossil fuel production.

Canada’s international reputation is often that of a moderate, diplomatic, peaceful country with strong institutions and a rules-based orientation. It is a member of NATO, the G7, the Commonwealth, La Francophonie, and many other international organizations. Peacekeeping has long been part of its global image, though the reality of its international role is broader and more complicated, including military alliances, trade diplomacy, development assistance, and Arctic sovereignty concerns.

Socially and politically, Canada is often seen as more centrist than the United States, though that can oversimplify things. Canadian politics includes serious debates over federalism, provincial rights, Indigenous sovereignty, immigration, housing affordability, energy policy, language, taxation, and social welfare. Regional divisions are especially important. Western alienation, Quebec nationalism, urban-rural differences, and northern governance issues all shape national politics in ways that outsiders often underestimate.

Canadian identity itself is somewhat paradoxical. It is often defined by both what Canada is and what it is not. Canadians may emphasize public health care, bilingualism, multiculturalism, social order, and a less overtly individualistic political culture. Yet Canada is not a single uniform society. It is better understood as a negotiated federation with multiple founding narratives, overlapping sovereignties, and strong regional cultures.

In symbolic terms, Canada is associated with the maple leaf, the red-and-white national flag, the beaver, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, and vast wilderness. But beyond these familiar images, the country is best understood as a complex modern state: wealthy but unequal, inclusive in aspiration but marked by colonial injustice, geographically immense but sparsely populated, culturally diverse yet regionally fragmented, and internationally admired while still grappling with unresolved internal questions.

If you want, I can also give you:
1. A version focused on Canadian history.
2. A version focused on culture and daily life.
3. A province-by-province breakdown.
4. A simpler school-style essay version.

Ice hockey holds a special symbolic place, though Canada is also deeply engaged with sports such as lacrosse, soccer, basketball, curling, and skiing.

Climate varies enormously across the country. Southern regions where most people live experience four distinct seasons, while northern areas can have extremely long, severe winters and short summers. Climate change has become a significant issue in Canada, especially in relation to wildfires, Arctic warming, melting permafrost, flooding, and changing ecosystems. This creates an especially sharp policy tension because Canada is both vulnerable to climate impacts and economically tied to fossil fuel production.

Canada’s international reputation is often that of a moderate, diplomatic, peaceful country with strong institutions and a rules-based orientation. It is a member of NATO, the G7, the Commonwealth, La Francophonie, and many other international organizations. Peacekeeping has long been part of its global image, though the reality of its international role is broader and more complicated, including military alliances, trade diplomacy, development assistance, and Arctic sovereignty concerns.

Socially and politically, Canada is often seen as more centrist than the United States, though that can oversimplify things. Canadian politics includes serious debates over federalism, provincial rights, Indigenous sovereignty, immigration, housing affordability, energy policy, language, taxation, and social welfare. Regional divisions are especially important. Western alienation, Quebec nationalism, urban-rural differences, and northern governance issues all shape national politics in ways that outsiders often underestimate.

Canadian identity itself is somewhat paradoxical. It is often defined by both what Canada is and what it is not. Canadians may emphasize public health care, bilingualism, multiculturalism, social order, and a less overtly individualistic political culture. Yet Canada is not a single uniform society. It is better understood as a negotiated federation with multiple founding narratives, overlapping sovereignties, and strong regional cultures.

In symbolic terms, Canada is associated with the maple leaf, the red-and-white national flag, the beaver, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, and vast wilderness. But beyond these familiar images, the country is best understood as a complex modern state: wealthy but unequal, inclusive in aspiration but marked by colonial injustice, geographically immense but sparsely populated, culturally diverse yet regionally fragmented, and internationally admired while still grappling with unresolved internal questions.
